{"step_by_step_thinking": "Optic atrophy is a sign of damage to the optic nerve, typically resulting from degeneration of retinal ganglion cell axons. It is commonly associated with conditions that affect the retina or its blood supply.

Option A: Retinitis pigmentosa - This is a hereditary retinal disease that leads to progressive degeneration of photoreceptors. It is associated with optic atrophy in later stages, as the degeneration extends to the retinal ganglion cells and their axons. Therefore, optic atrophy can be seen in retinitis pigmentosa.

Option B: Methanol poisoning - Methanol toxicity can cause optic neuropathy, leading to optic atrophy due to metabolic disturbances and direct toxicity to the optic nerve. Thus, optic atrophy is seen in methanol poisoning.

Option C: Central retinal artery occlusion (CRAO) - CRAO results in ischemia of the retina, leading to optic nerve damage and subsequent optic atrophy. This is well-documented in the literature as a cause of optic atrophy due to ischemic injury to the retinal ganglion cells and optic nerve.

Option D: Polypoidal choroidal vasculopathy (PCV) - This is a choroidal vascular disorder primarily affecting the choroid and involving neovascular polyps. It primarily presents with subretinal neovascularization and exudative changes, but does not typically involve the optic nerve or retinal ganglion cells directly. There is no direct link between PCV and optic atrophy. The pathology is confined to the choroid and retina, without significant axonal degeneration or ischemia affecting the optic nerve.

Therefore, optic atrophy is not typically seen in polypoidal choroidal vasculopathy.", "answer_choice": "D"}
